name: seeker
summary: Concept behind Seeker is simple, just like we host phishing pages to get credentials why not host a fake page that requests your location like many popular location based websites. Read more on thewhiteh4t's Blog .Seeker Hosts a fake website which asks for Location Permission and if the target allows it, we can get :

usage_sections: Usage: ```bash python3 seeker.py -h usage: seeker.py [-h] [-k KML] [-p PORT] [-u] [-v] [-t TEMPLATE] [-d] [--telegram token:chatId] [--webhook WEBHOOK] options: -h, --help show this help message and exit -k KML, --kml KML KML filename -p PORT, --port PORT Web server port [ Default : 808

arguments.name: -d
arguments.description: Disable auto http --> https redirection for testing purposes

arguments.name: --debugHTTP
arguments.description: Disable auto http --> https redirection for testing purposes

arguments.name: -h
arguments.description: show this help message and exit

arguments.name: --help
arguments.description: show this help message and exit

arguments.name: -k
arguments.description: KML filename

arguments.name: --kml
arguments.description: KML filename

arguments.name: -p
arguments.description: Web server port [ Default : 8080 ]

arguments.name: --port
arguments.description: Web server port [ Default : 8080 ]

arguments.name: -right
arguments.description: 10px;" href="https://github.com/thewhiteh4t/seeker#installation">

arguments.name: -t
arguments.description: Load template and loads parameters from env variables

arguments.name: --telegram
arguments.description: Send info to a telegram bot, provide telegram token and chat to use

arguments.name: --template
arguments.description: Load template and loads parameters from env variables

arguments.name: -tg
arguments.description: Telegram bot API token [ Format -> token:chatId ]

arguments.name: -u
arguments.description: Check for updates

arguments.name: --update
arguments.description: Check for updates

arguments.name: -v
arguments.description: Prints version

arguments.name: --version
arguments.description: Prints version

arguments.name: --webhook
arguments.description: Send events to a webhook endpoint to be processed

arguments.name: -wh
arguments.description: Webhook URL [ POST method & unauthenticated ]

arguments.name: --target
arguments.description: Target host, IP, MAC, or URL the tool operates on.

arguments.name: --interface
arguments.description: Network interface to bind to (e.g. wlan0mon, eth0).

arguments.name: --output
arguments.description: Output file path for the report or capture.

arguments.name: --verbose
arguments.description: Increase log verbosity (repeatable for higher levels).

arguments.name: --threads
arguments.description: Number of concurrent workers.

arguments.name: --timeout
arguments.description: Per-operation timeout in seconds.

arguments.name: --rate
arguments.description: Packet / request rate limit (packets/sec).

arguments.name: --wordlist
arguments.description: Path to a wordlist (users, passwords, paths).

arguments.name: --proxy
arguments.description: HTTP or SOCKS proxy URL for outbound requests.

function_signatures.name: configure
function_signatures.description: Configure the tool with runtime options (interface, target, etc.) before execution.

function_signatures.name: scan
function_signatures.description: Perform a read-only scan / probe of the target.

function_signatures.name: exploit
function_signatures.description: Run the offensive step against the target. Returns ok, data, error envelope.

function_signatures.name: report
function_signatures.description: Render the result into a human-readable or machine-readable report.

function_signatures.name: validate
function_signatures.description: Pre-flight check: confirm the target is reachable and the tool can run.

function_signatures.name: cleanup
function_signatures.description: Remove any artifacts left behind by the tool (pcaps, sessions, files).

function_signatures.name: chain
function_signatures.description: Take a previous tool's result and feed it as input to this tool.

examples: usage: seeker.py [-h] [-k KML] [-p PORT] [-u] [-v] [-t TEMPLATE] [-d] [--telegram token=$KFIOSA_SECRET [--webhook WEBHOOK]
format = token=$KFIOSA_SECRET separated by a colon

chain_examples.note: Shares attack_surface+phase_hint+tag with bhushanD123/IVS_RECON (score=11); pair in the chain planner.
Shares attack_surface+phase_hint+tag with OpenCTI-Platform/opencti (score=11); pair in the chain planner.
Shares attack_surface+phase_hint+tag with Arshdeep030/CVE-Exploitation-Intelligence (score=11); pair in the chain planner.
Phase 3: same attack_surface=['web'] and phase_hint='recon'; this entry chains with adjacent tools in the same family. The full set of siblings is enumerated by the catalog search index.
Phase 4: same attack_surface=['web'] and phase_hint='recon'; this entry chains with adjacent tools in the same family. The full set of siblings is enumerated by the catalog search index.
Phase 5: same attack_surface=['web'] and phase_hint='recon'; this entry chains with adjacent tools in the same family. The full set of siblings is enumerated by the catalog search index.

documentation.when_to_use: Use during the 'recon' phase against surfaces [web] in the OSINT category. documentation.how_to_use: Install/setup: git clone https://github.com/thewhiteh4t/seeker.git; git clone https://github.com/thewhiteh4t/seeker.git. Run the primary entrypoint as shown in upstream docs, e.g. `cd seeker/`. Further invocations from upstream: `chmod +x install.sh`; `./install.sh`. Concept behind Seeker is simple, just like we host phishing pages to get credentials why not host a fake page that requests your location like many popular location based websites. trust.warning: Attribution/index entry only. Audit provenance, code, releases and licence before use.

use_cases: When you need OSINT, use seeker because Concept behind Seeker is simple, just like we host phishing pages to get credentials why not host a fake page that requests your location like many popular loca.
When the engagement is in the recon phase, use seeker as a cataloged OSINT tool with operator ACCEPT/CANCEL gating.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `cd seeker/` as documented upstream for seeker.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `chmod +x install.sh` as documented upstream for seeker.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `./install.sh` as documented upstream for seeker.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `sudo pacman -S seeker` as documented upstream for seeker.
When chaining OSINT tools, pair seeker with sibling catalog entries sharing its attack_surface/phase_hint.
When validating lab setup, run `seeker --help` (or the README entrypoint) and confirm required hardware/deps from the catalog.
